title: Anemia and Polycythemia
authors: Thomas G. DeLoughery, Dan L. Longo

items.subtopic: Anemia and Polycythemia | NORMAL RED CELL PRODUCTION (part 1)
items.title: EPO Regulation of Red Cell Production
items.content: Erythropoietin (EPO) is the primary hormone regulating red cell production. It maintains committed erythroid progenitor cells, preventing apoptosis in their absence. EPO levels increase with decreased hemoglobin concentration to stimulate red cell production.
keyPoints: EPO is essential for maintaining erythroid progenitor cells and preventing programmed cell death (apoptosis).
Normal plasma EPO level: 10–25 U/L.
Plasma EPO levels increase when hemoglobin falls below 100–120 g/L (10–12 g/dL).
EPO has a half-life of 6–9 hours and acts on marrow erythroid precursors to stimulate proliferation and maturation.
items.reference: Anemia and Polycythemia · Anemia and Polycythemia | NORMAL RED CELL PRODUCTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: Anemia and Polycythemia | NORMAL RED CELL PRODUCTION (part 1)
items.title: Mechanism of EPO Production
items.content: Erythropoietin production is regulated by tissue oxygenation, primarily through hypoxia-inducible factor-1 alpha (HIF-1α). In low oxygen conditions, HIF-1α stabilizes and promotes EPO gene expression.
keyPoints: EPO is produced mainly in the kidney's peritubular capillary cells.
Hypoxia-inducible factor-1 α (HIF-1α) regulates EPO production; it is stabilized when oxygen levels are low, inducing EPO gene expression.
Impaired O₂ delivery to kidneys can increase EPO production due to hypoxia.
items.reference: Anemia and Polycythemia · Anemia and Polycythemia | NORMAL RED CELL PRODUCTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: Anemia and Polycythemia | NORMAL RED CELL PRODUCTION (part 1)
items.title: Erythropoiesis Process
items.content: The process of erythropoiesis involves the proliferation and maturation of erythroid precursors in response to EPO, requiring adequate nutrients like iron for effective red cell production.
keyPoints: Pronormoblasts undergo 4–5 divisions to produce 16–32 mature red cells.
Erythropoiesis can increase four- to fivefold within a 1- to 2-week period with EPO stimulation, given adequate nutrients.
Normal daily replacement of circulating red cells is 0.8–1%, as they have an average lifespan of 100–120 days.
items.reference: Anemia and Polycythemia · Anemia and Polycythemia | NORMAL RED CELL PRODUCTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: NORMAL RED CELL PRODUCTION (part 2) | SIGNS AND SYMPTOMS OF ANEMIA
items.title: Diagnostic Approach to Anemia: History and Physical Examination
items.content: A comprehensive history is crucial in diagnosing anemia, focusing on previous episodes, therapies, family history, and menstrual history for menstruating individuals. The physical exam should assess signs of anemia's consequences like cardiac murmurs and potential causes such as splenomegaly or blood in stool.
keyPoints: Ask about past anemia episodes, iron therapy, transfusions, family history, and blood donation.
In menstruating individuals, inquire about period duration, pad/tampon use, and clot size.
Physical exam should check for cardiac murmurs, splenomegaly, or blood in stool.
items.reference: Anemia and Polycythemia · NORMAL RED CELL PRODUCTION (part 2) | SIGNS AND SYMPTOMS OF ANEMIA

items.subtopic: NORMAL RED CELL PRODUCTION (part 2) | SIGNS AND SYMPTOMS OF ANEMIA
items.title: Clinical Manifestations and Compensatory Mechanisms of Anemia
items.content: Patients with chronic anemia may exhibit surprisingly low hemoglobin levels due to compensatory mechanisms. Symptoms like tiredness, shortness of breath, paleness, and resting tachycardia are common, while those with atherosclerosis might experience ischemic symptoms. Nutritional deficiencies can cause specific symptoms such as pica in iron deficiency or neuropathy in B12 deficiency.
keyPoints: Chronic anemia patients may tolerate low hemoglobin due to compensatory mechanisms.
Common signs include tiredness, shortness of breath, paleness, and resting tachycardia.
Atherosclerotic patients might have ischemic symptoms like angina or transient ischemic attacks/strokes.
Nutritional deficiencies can cause specific symptoms: pica in iron deficiency, neuropathy in B12 deficiency.
items.reference: Anemia and Polycythemia · NORMAL RED CELL PRODUCTION (part 2) | SIGNS AND SYMPTOMS OF ANEMIA

items.subtopic: COMPENSATION FOR ANEMIA | LABORATORY TESTING
items.title: Compensatory Mechanisms for Anemia and Their Clinical Implications
items.content: The body compensates for anemia through increased cardiac output, elevated levels of 2,3-DPG, and increased plasma volume. These mechanisms help maintain oxygen delivery to tissues despite low hemoglobin levels.
keyPoints: Increased cardiac output occurs within minutes to compensate for lower hemoglobin by improving tissue oxygen delivery.
Elevated 2,3-DPG decreases oxygen affinity for hemoglobin over hours to days, enhancing tissue oxygenation.
Plasma volume increases over weeks to preserve cardiac output and blood pressure, potentially leading to edema if the heart is overwhelmed.
items.reference: Anemia and Polycythemia · COMPENSATION FOR ANEMIA | LABORATORY TESTING

items.subtopic: COMPENSATION FOR ANEMIA | LABORATORY TESTING
items.title: Laboratory Detection of Anemia via Complete Blood Count (CBC)
items.content: Anemia is often detected through low hemoglobin and hematocrit levels in a CBC. Electronic cell counters measure red cell size, number, and hemoglobin concentration to derive indices like MCV.
keyPoints: Hematocrit calculation: Hematocrit = {Red cell number/liter x MCV}{10}.
Mean corpuscular volume (MCV) is a key index used in red cell analysis.
Normal variations of hemoglobin and hematocrit exist with age, which are documented in reference materials.
items.reference: Anemia and Polycythemia · COMPENSATION FOR ANEMIA | LABORATORY TESTING

items.subtopic: BLOOD SMEAR | BURR CELLS | ELLIPTOCYTES | HOWELL-JOLLY BODIES | HYPOCHROMIA/MICROCYTOSIS
items.title: Blood Smear as a Diagnostic Tool in Anemia
items.content: The blood smear provides critical insights into the morphology of red cells, aiding in diagnosing various anemias. Variations in cell size (anisocytosis) and shape (poikilocytosis), along with specific morphological changes like polychromasia, can indicate underlying conditions such as EPO stimulation or bone marrow damage.
keyPoints: Anisocytosis correlates with increases in red cell distribution width (RDW).
Poikilocytosis suggests defects in red cell maturation or fragmentation.
Polychromasia indicates premature reticulocyte release due to EPO stimulation or bone marrow damage.
items.reference: Anemia and Polycythemia · BLOOD SMEAR | BURR CELLS | ELLIPTOCYTES | HOWELL-JOLLY BODIES | HYPOCHROMIA/MICROCYTOSIS

items.subtopic: BLOOD SMEAR | BURR CELLS | ELLIPTOCYTES | HOWELL-JOLLY BODIES | HYPOCHROMIA/MICROCYTOSIS
items.title: Significance of Hypochromia and Microcytosis in Anemia
items.content: Hypochromia and microcytosis are indicative of inadequate hemoglobinization, commonly seen in conditions like iron deficiency, thalassemia, or sideroblastic anemia. These morphological changes on a blood smear help pinpoint the underlying cause of anemia.
keyPoints: Hypochromia is characterized by larger areas of central pallor than normal.
Microcytosis indicates smaller red cells with inadequate hemoglobinization.
Common causes include iron deficiency, thalassemia, and sideroblastic anemia.
items.reference: Anemia and Polycythemia · BLOOD SMEAR | BURR CELLS | ELLIPTOCYTES | HOWELL-JOLLY BODIES | HYPOCHROMIA/MICROCYTOSIS

items.subtopic: MACRO-OVALOCYTES | NUCLEATED RED BLOOD CELLS
items.title: Identifying Causes of Macro-Ovalocytes and Nucleated Red Blood Cells
items.content: Macro-ovalocytes are larger, oval-shaped red cells due to disrupted DNA synthesis from B12/folate deficiency, drugs, or bone marrow disorders. Nucleated red blood cells indicate asplenia, severe marrow stress (e.g., massive bleeding, hemolysis), or bone marrow architecture disruption.
keyPoints: Macro-ovalocytes caused by B12/folate deficiency, certain drugs, or bone marrow disorders.
Nucleated red blood cells seen with asplenia, severe marrow stress, and disrupted marrow architecture.
items.reference: Anemia and Polycythemia · MACRO-OVALOCYTES | NUCLEATED RED BLOOD CELLS | SCHISTOCYTES | SICKLE CELLS | SPHEROCYTES | SPUR CELLS

items.subtopic: SCHISTOCYTES | SICKLE CELLS
items.title: Recognizing Schistocytes and Sickle Cells in Hemolytic Disorders
items.content: Schistocytes are red cell fragments from vascular blockage or external forces, seen in conditions like thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura. Sickle cells indicate hemoglobin defects, primarily sickle cell anemia.
keyPoints: Schistocytes result from physical disruption of red cells by platelets/fibrin strands or external forces.
Sickle cells are indicative of hemoglobin defects, most commonly seen in sickle cell anemia.
items.reference: Anemia and Polycythemia · MACRO-OVALOCYTES | NUCLEATED RED BLOOD CELLS | SCHISTOCYTES | SICKLE CELLS | SPHEROCYTES | SPUR CELLS

items.subtopic: TARGET CELLS | THE RETICULOCYTE COUNT | BONE MARROW EXAM
items.title: Understanding Target Cells and Their Clinical Implications
items.content: Target cells, which appear due to redundant red cell membranes, are indicative of conditions like thalassemia (due to less hemoglobin) or liver disease (excess membrane). Teardrop-shaped cells suggest disruption in marrow architecture or severe iron deficiency.
keyPoints: Target cells indicate thalassemia or liver disease.
Teardrop cells are associated with disrupted marrow architecture or severe iron deficiency.
items.reference: Anemia and Polycythemia · TARGET CELLS | THE RETICULOCYTE COUNT | BONE MARROW EXAM

items.subtopic: TARGET CELLS | THE RETICULOCYTE COUNT | BONE MARROW EXAM
items.title: Reticulocyte Count and Its Clinical Significance
items.content: The reticulocyte count, corrected for hematocrit, helps differentiate between increased red cell destruction and impaired production. The formula Corrected Reticulocyte Count = Measured Reticulocyte Count × (Patient Hematocrit/45%) is used to adjust the percentage based on hematocrit levels.
keyPoints: Reticulocytes are young red cells with detectable mRNA for about 24 hours post-release.
Corrected reticulocyte count = Measured reticulocyte count × (Patient hematocrit/45%).
Absolute reticulocyte number = Reticulocyte count/100 × Red blood cell number.
items.reference: Anemia and Polycythemia · TARGET CELLS | THE RETICULOCYTE COUNT | BONE MARROW EXAM

items.subtopic: TARGET CELLS | THE RETICULOCYTE COUNT | BONE MARROW EXAM
items.title: When to Perform a Bone Marrow Examination
items.content: Bone marrow examination is crucial for diagnosing conditions like aplastic anemia or myelodysplasia. Indications include circulating immature cells, severe pancytopenia, very low reticulocyte counts (<0.1%), and evidence of marrow infiltration.
keyPoints: Normal myeloid-to-erythroid ratio is 2.5:1 (range 2:1–5:1).
Bone marrow examination indicated for circulating immature cells, severe pancytopenia, very low reticulocyte counts (<0.1%), and evidence of marrow infiltration.
Normal bone marrow cellularity is ~50%.
items.reference: Anemia and Polycythemia · TARGET CELLS | THE RETICULOCYTE COUNT | BONE MARROW EXAM

items.subtopic: ANEMIA DEFINITION AND CLASSIFICATION
items.title: How Anemia is Classified by Red Cell Size and Its Diagnostic Implications
items.content: Anemia can be classified based on the mean corpuscular volume (MCV) of red blood cells, which helps guide diagnostic workup. Microcytic anemia involves smaller MCV due to impaired hemoglobin production, while macrocytic anemia involves larger MCV often related to DNA synthesis defects.
keyPoints: Anemia is defined as blood counts below normal for a given population.
Classification by red cell size: microcytic (smaller MCV), normocytic (normal MCV), and macrocytic (larger MCV).
Microcytic anemia causes include thalassemia, iron deficiency, anemia of chronic disease, and sideroblastic anemias.
Macrocytic anemia can be due to $B_{12}$ or folate deficiency, among other processes.
items.reference: Anemia and Polycythemia · ANEMIA DEFINITION AND CLASSIFICATION

items.subtopic: ANEMIA DEFINITION AND CLASSIFICATION
items.title: Understanding Anemia Mechanisms through Reticulocyte Counts
items.content: The absolute reticulocyte count is a crucial first indicator of anemia mechanism. Elevated reticulocytes suggest increased red cell loss or destruction, whereas low counts indicate decreased production.
keyPoints: Reticulocyte count helps determine the mechanism of anemia.
Elevated reticulocytes imply hemolysis or blood loss.
Low reticulocyte counts suggest impaired red cell production.
items.reference: Anemia and Polycythemia · ANEMIA DEFINITION AND CLASSIFICATION

items.subtopic: Anemia and Polycythemia | NORMAL RED CELL PRODUCTION (part 1)
items.question: Which transcription factors are essential for the development of erythroid cells from a common progenitor?
items.explanation: The development of erythroid cells from a common progenitor requires the expression of GATA-1 and FOG-1 transcription factors.
items.reference: Anemia and Polycythemia · Anemia and Polycythemia | NORMAL RED CELL PRODUCTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: Anemia and Polycythemia | NORMAL RED CELL PRODUCTION (part 1)
items.question: What is the primary regulatory hormone for red cell production?
items.explanation: Erythropoietin (EPO) is the primary regulatory hormone required for the maintenance of committed erythroid progenitor cells.
items.reference: Anemia and Polycythemia · Anemia and Polycythemia | NORMAL RED CELL PRODUCTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: Anemia and Polycythemia | NORMAL RED CELL PRODUCTION (part 1)
items.question: What happens to HIF-1α in the presence of sufficient oxygen?
options: It is stabilized and translocates to the nucleus
It is hydroxylated, ubiquitinated, and degraded
It upregulates EPO gene expression directly
It binds to erythroid progenitor cells
items.explanation: In the presence of sufficient oxygen, HIF-1α is hydroxylated at a key proline, allowing it to be ubiquitinated and degraded via the proteasome pathway.
items.reference: Anemia and Polycythemia · Anemia and Polycythemia | NORMAL RED CELL PRODUCTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: Anemia and Polycythemia | NORMAL RED CELL PRODUCTION (part 1)
items.question: What is the typical lifespan of a mature red blood cell?
items.explanation: The average lifespan of a mature red blood cell is 100–120 days.
items.reference: Anemia and Polycythemia · Anemia and Polycythemia | NORMAL RED CELL PRODUCTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: NORMAL RED CELL PRODUCTION (part 2) | SIGNS AND SYMPTOMS OF ANEMIA
items.question: Which of the following is NOT a recommended initial step in diagnosing anemia?
options: Taking a detailed history including previous episodes and therapy.
Assessing for symptoms like diarrhea that can lead to anemia.
Performing a comprehensive metabolic panel as the first test.
Reviewing menstrual history in individuals who menstruate.
items.explanation: A comprehensive metabolic panel is not listed as an initial step in diagnosing anemia; instead, history and symptom assessment are emphasized.
items.reference: Anemia and Polycythemia · NORMAL RED CELL PRODUCTION (part 2) | SIGNS AND SYMPTOMS OF ANEMIA

items.subtopic: NORMAL RED CELL PRODUCTION (part 2) | SIGNS AND SYMPTOMS OF ANEMIA
items.question: What does the reticulocyte count assess in the evaluation of anemia?
options: The number of new red cells produced by the bone marrow.
The presence of blood in the stool.
items.explanation: Reticulocyte count assesses the production of new red cells, indicating bone marrow function.
items.reference: Anemia and Polycythemia · NORMAL RED CELL PRODUCTION (part 2) | SIGNS AND SYMPTOMS OF ANEMIA

items.subtopic: NORMAL RED CELL PRODUCTION (part 2) | SIGNS AND SYMPTOMS OF ANEMIA
items.question: Which clinical sign is NOT typically associated with anemia?
items.explanation: Anemia is typically associated with low oxygen delivery, not increased blood pressure.
items.reference: Anemia and Polycythemia · NORMAL RED CELL PRODUCTION (part 2) | SIGNS AND SYMPTOMS OF ANEMIA

items.subtopic: COMPENSATION FOR ANEMIA | LABORATORY TESTING
items.question: Which compensatory mechanism for anemia occurs within minutes?
options: Decrease in oxygen affinity for hemoglobin
items.explanation: The first compensatory mechanism is an increase in cardiac output which can occur within minutes.
items.reference: Anemia and Polycythemia · COMPENSATION FOR ANEMIA | LABORATORY TESTING

items.subtopic: COMPENSATION FOR ANEMIA | LABORATORY TESTING
items.question: What is the role of 2,3-DPG in compensating for anemia?
options: It increases oxygen affinity for hemoglobin.
It decreases oxygen affinity for hemoglobin.
items.explanation: 2,3-DPG decreases oxygen affinity for hemoglobin, leading to more oxygen delivery in tissues.
items.reference: Anemia and Polycythemia · COMPENSATION FOR ANEMIA | LABORATORY TESTING

items.subtopic: COMPENSATION FOR ANEMIA | LABORATORY TESTING
items.question: Over what time frame does plasma volume increase as a compensatory mechanism for anemia?
items.explanation: Plasma volume increases over weeks as a compensatory mechanism for anemia.
items.reference: Anemia and Polycythemia · COMPENSATION FOR ANEMIA | LABORATORY TESTING

options: Hereditary elliptocytosis or severe iron deficiency

items.explanation: Howell-Jolly bodies are seen when the spleen is missing or not functioning properly.

Physical disruption by blockage in blood vessels
items.explanation: Sickle-shaped blood cells are primarily caused by hemoglobin defects, most commonly seen in sickle cell anemia.

items.question: Which of the following is a characteristic feature of target cells?
options: They are seen with disruption of marrow architecture.
They have redundant red cell membranes.
items.explanation: Target cells can be seen where there is a redundant red cell membrane, as in thalassemia or liver disease.

items.question: What does the presence of teardrop-shaped cells most often indicate?
items.explanation: Teardrop-shaped cells are most often seen with disruption of marrow architecture or severe iron deficiency.

items.question: What is the purpose of correcting the reticulocyte count for hematocrit?
options: To adjust for changes in red blood cell production.
To account for variations in patient age.
To correct for apparent increases due to low hematocrit levels.
items.explanation: The reticulocyte count needs to be corrected for the hematocrit because the percentage will appear higher with decreasing blood counts, even if the absolute count has not increased.

items.subtopic: Anemia and Polycythemia | NORMAL RED CELL PRODUCTION (part 1)
items.assertion: The biconcave shape of red blood cells maximizes oxygen delivery.
items.reason: This shape provides the greatest surface area for a given volume, facilitating efficient gas exchange.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason is the correct explanation of the assertion.
items.reference: Anemia and Polycythemia · Anemia and Polycythemia | NORMAL RED CELL PRODUCTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: Anemia and Polycythemia | NORMAL RED CELL PRODUCTION (part 1)
items.assertion: HIF-1α directly binds to erythroid progenitor cells.
items.reason: HIF-1α is involved in the regulation of EPO gene expression under low oxygen conditions.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; HIF-1α does not bind to erythroid progenitor cells but regulates EPO gene expression.
items.reference: Anemia and Polycythemia · Anemia and Polycythemia | NORMAL RED CELL PRODUCTION (part 1)

items.subtopic: NORMAL RED CELL PRODUCTION (part 2) | SIGNS AND SYMPTOMS OF ANEMIA
items.assertion: Patients with gradually developing anemia may tolerate low hemoglobin levels.
items.reason: Compensatory mechanisms allow individuals to adapt to reduced oxygen delivery over time.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why patients can tolerate low hemoglobin levels.
items.reference: Anemia and Polycythemia · NORMAL RED CELL PRODUCTION (part 2) | SIGNS AND SYMPTOMS OF ANEMIA

items.subtopic: COMPENSATION FOR ANEMIA | LABORATORY TESTING
items.assertion: Patients with limited cardiac reserve will have symptoms of anemia at a higher hematocrit.
items.reason: This is because their compensatory increase in cardiac output is less effective.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why patients with limited cardiac reserve experience symptoms at higher hematocrit levels.
items.reference: Anemia and Polycythemia · COMPENSATION FOR ANEMIA | LABORATORY TESTING

items.subtopic: TARGET CELLS | THE RETICULOCYTE COUNT | BONE MARROW EXAM
items.assertion: A bone marrow examination is necessary when there are circulating immature cells.
items.reason: Circulating immature cells suggest the possibility of marrow infiltration or generalized marrow dysfunction.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why a bone marrow examination is necessary in this context.
items.reference: Anemia and Polycythemia · TARGET CELLS | THE RETICULOCYTE COUNT | BONE MARROW EXAM

items.subtopic: TARGET CELLS | THE RETICULOCYTE COUNT | BONE MARROW EXAM
items.assertion: The normal bone marrow is approximately 50% cellular.
items.reason: This percentage includes both hematopoietic cells and fat cells, with the latter being roughly equivalent to the patient's age.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the composition of normal bone marrow.
items.reference: Anemia and Polycythemia · TARGET CELLS | THE RETICULOCYTE COUNT | BONE MARROW EXAM

items.subtopic: ANEMIA DEFINITION AND CLASSIFICATION
items.assertion: Iron deficiency leads to microcytic anemia.
items.reason: Less hemoglobin results in smaller red cells.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why iron deficiency causes microcytic anemia.
items.reference: Anemia and Polycythemia · ANEMIA DEFINITION AND CLASSIFICATION
